title: Handbook of Error-Correcting Codes
abstract: Barcode scans, clear phone calls, reliable data storage, satellite communication, and large-scale quantum computation are all made possible by error correction. We present a handbook version of The Error Correction Zoo, a curated reference of methods for protecting classical or quantum information from errors during storage and transmission. The handbook includes descriptions of these error-correcting codes and a classification according to the symbols they use. It also catalogues relations among codes and related objects such as sphere packings, lattices, designs, groups, and classical and quantum phases of matter. The collection is intended both as a rigorous reference and as a practical aid for tracing the web of code relationships and uncovering new connections.
summary: This paper introduces 'The Error Correction Zoo,' a comprehensive, structured reference manual for all known classical and quantum error-correcting codes. It organizes these codes taxonomically based on their mathematical support—ranging from simple binary systems to complex bosonic or group structures. This resource aims to be the definitive guide for researchers needing to select appropriate protective schemes for various physical implementations.
main_problem: The core problem is developing a comprehensive, structured reference (the Error Correction Zoo) to catalog methods for protecting classical or quantum information from errors during storage, transmission, and computation.
main_result: The authors propose a highly structured framework organizing codes into Domains (Classical/Quantum) and Kingdoms based on underlying mathematical structures, providing both a rigorous reference and a practical aid for code discovery.
method: The methodology involves creating a hierarchical database structure (the Zoo) using YAML data files, processed through an in-memory object model to generate structured HTML outputs.
model_or_system: The system models information protection across classical and quantum channels. Codes are classified based on their physical support: qubits, qudits, bosonic modes, or algebraic structures like groups and lattices.
key_observables: Code distance ($d$), code rate, threshold values ($	ext{threshold}$), and capacity thresholds.
important_parameters: Alphabet size (binary, $\mathfrak{q}$-ary), physical dimensions (qubits, qudits, bosonic modes), and algebraic parameters defining the underlying structure.
main_assumptions: The selection of an optimal code is case-by-case; quantum error correction must contend with limitations like the no-cloning theorem compared to classical systems.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the EC Zoo structure, showing Domains containing Kingdoms organized by alphabet/physical space, and depict the data processing pipeline from YAML input to final HTML output.
paper_structure: The paper is structured around presenting the conceptual framework of the Error Correction Zoo. It details the taxonomy (Domains $ightarrow$ Kingdoms), the mathematical relationships between codes, and the computational pipeline used to maintain this complex reference resource.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial meta-framework for quantum information theorists, mapping out the landscape of existing error correction techniques by linking them explicitly to underlying algebraic or physical structures (e.g., lattices, groups).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: The EC Zoo consists of three Domains, with each Domain consisting of several Kingdoms. Codes are primarily organized by the alphabet (for quantum, physical space) used to define the codes; each alphabet is a Kingdom (diamonds). Horizontal red arrows show correspondences between Kingdoms in different Domains; e.g., qubit codes are quantum analogues of binary codes. Vertical arrows relate Kingdoms to each other; e.g., the Qubit Kingdom descends from the Modular-qudit Kingdom because qubits are a special case of qudits.
FIG. 3: Elements that appear in a code graph

FIG. 2: The EC Zoo processing pipeline. The code data is maintained as a repository of structured YAML files, which are both human-editable and machine-readable. The files are parsed into an in-memory object model, with JavaScript objects repre- senting the zoo instance, code instances as well as lists, domains, and kingdoms. The objects expose methods for computed properties, such as finding the parent kingdom of a code, exposing an API that enables powerful processing with custom code. Our code can generate our HTML website (https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/) , static or interactive code graphs (https://errorcorrectionzoo.org/code_graph) , or even a print-ready handbook.
